agent_raw:
The evidence does not justify automated approvals and faster denials in oncology prior-authorization. Three structural problems undermine the retrospective validation.

First, the training labels are final authorization outcomes under payer policy at the time — not ground truth about medical appropriateness. Oncology guidelines changed repeatedly during the study period as biosimilars entered the market and some therapies moved billing pathways. The model learns to reproduce historical policy decisions, which will diverge from evolving standards. Automated denials based on policy-lagged model outputs create meaningful patient safety risk.

Second, the vendor reports only aggregate agreement metrics. No breakdown by cancer type, line of therapy, or policy vintage is provided. Agreement that looks strong in aggregate can mask serious failures in the subgroups that matter most: newly approved regimens, later-line therapies, or cases where policy changed mid-study.

Third, urgent oncology cases handled through expedited physician-to-physician review outside the standard queue are underrepresented or poorly labeled in the historical data. These are the highest-stakes cases — rapid progressors, patients needing treatment before insurance processing delays — and the model has no adequate training signal for them.

The case for deploying auto-denials is especially weak given these issues. An incorrect automatic denial in oncology can delay time-sensitive cancer treatment. That harm is not symmetric with a wrongly approved claim, but agreement metrics do not reflect this asymmetry.

The committee should require prospective validation on stable regimens before any auto-approval deployment, and should exclude auto-denial from the initial scope entirely.
all_issues_raised: Training labels encode payer policy, not stable medical appropriateness
Aggregate agreement metrics hide cancer-type and therapy-line performance variation
Urgent expedited-review cases underrepresented in training data
Automated denials carry asymmetric patient harm not reflected in agreement metrics
